pattern: Treating indices as graph nodes — neighbours being the adjacent indices and all indices sharing the current value — turns the minimum-jumps question into a breadth-first search whose layer number is the answer.
hints: Each index can move to its neighbours and to any other index holding the same value. That is a graph; the fewest jumps is a shortest path on it.
Breadth-first search explores positions in order of jump count, so the first time you reach the last index you already have the minimum.

Pre-group indices by value. BFS from index 0; when you pop an index, enqueue i-1, i+1, and every index with the same value, then clear that value's group so its edges are never re-traversed — that bound keeps it linear.

edge_inputs.description: a single value-jump reaches the end directly
all identical values let one jump finish
no repeated values forces step-by-step walking
must walk backward to use a value-jump

stress.description: long array over a small value range so large same-value groups appear
stress.note: small range stresses the group-clearing optimisation
